data.title: U.S.-UK Is The First New Trade Deal, How Did We Do?
data.description:
Introduction

The morning of May 8th, a trade deal was signed between the United States and the United Kingdom. This comes as welcome news for the markets, which have been turbulent since "Liberation Day," or the unveiling of global proposed tariff rates.

There are too many moving parts to keep track of in trade policy, so I recommend taking a look at this tariff tracker from Bloomberg, as it does a great job of keeping everything organized and legible.

This deal is the first of many promises to come by the Trump administration, who famously (or infamously, depending on your political lean) said that they would do "90 deals in 90 days."

Now a little over 30 days in, and the results are beginning to flow, with the word that an agreement with India and the EU are coming soon as well.

The U.S.-UK Special Relationship

It's important to start any discussion of Anglo-American trade relations by noting out that the U.S. has historically had a very close relationship with the UK, which has historically been called the "special relationship." The phrase comes from a post-WW2 speech by Winston Churchill, then prime minister of the UK, but the partnership more recently blossomed in the 1980s, when then-Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher and President Ronald Reagan built out the neoliberal world order.

Flash forward to today, and the language being used by both sides still indicates that this relationship is important to both countries. In speaking about this deal, President Trump called Britain the U.S.' oldest ally, "just about." (Sorry, France.)

Because trade deals can't be enacted with EU countries without the approval of Brussels, and can only be done at the bloc level, the UK makes perfect sense to achieve the first deal, given this special relationship.

During the May 8th press conference in the UK, Prime Minister Kier Starmer evoked this phrase again:

As VE Day reminds us, the UK has no greater ally than the United States, so I am delighted that eight decades on, under President Trump the special relationship remains a force for economic and national security.

The Deal Has Been A Long Time Coming

There is an important timeline to discuss here that sets up some context. Prior to Brexit, the leaving of the European Union by the United Kingdom, the UK's trade relationship with the U.S. was governed by EU trade rules. Within the EU, member countries are forbidden from doing their own trade deals with external nations, and outsiders like the U.S. were made to do EU-wide deals. This falls in line with the EU "single market" doctrine, which aims to treat the entirety of the EU as one trade bloc externally, with free economic movement internally.

This is not dissimilar to how the U.S. operates, with the federal government oversees and enforcing intrastate commerce, and trade deals with foreign nations only able to take place at the national level. It would be very odd if California tried to make a trade deal with Mexico that excluded Arizona and Texas, for example.

While Brexit was a long-time coming from the referendum that triggered it in 2016, the country didn't leave the EU until 2020. Once it left in late January of that year, the UK attempted to negotiate new trade deals with its closest trading partners, like the U.S. However, no progress was made.

By the start of 2024, the UK still hadn't built out a U.S.-UK deal, and Kemi Badenoch, the leader of the Tories, the UK's center-right party, began to say that it was the U.S. holding up a deal, claiming the Biden administration had "no appetite for trade deals."

There's a Lot of Trade Already

Prior to this deal on May 8th, the U.S. was already the UK's largest trading partner, and accounted for nearly-18% of total UK trade, even without a Biden-era deal. On the subject, the British government writes:

Total trade in goods and services (exports plus imports) between the UK and United States was £314.6 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024, an increase of 3.9% or £11.9 billion in current prices from the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023. Of this £314.6 billion:

• Total UK exports to United States amounted to £196.3 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024 (an increase of 4.3% or £8.2 billion in current prices, compared to the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023);

• Total UK imports from United States amounted to £118.3 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024 (an increase of 3.2% or £3.7 billion in current prices, compared to the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023).

On the U.S. side, the UK is our 7th largest export destination, very close to the amount Germany and South Korea import from us. Interestingly enough, the UK is one of few countries that we have a trade surplus with, where they import more than they export. Trade has been growing YoY for every year over the last decade bar 2020, but that was a global issue.

Now that we have a new trade deal in hand, that begs the question: is it any good? If so, for whom?

U.S.-UK Trade Deal Details

Context: The full trade deal has not been inked yet, and so this announcement is more like a framework than a fully fledged trade deal. It will still be enforceable, but expect new details to emerge as the days and weeks go on.

As soon as the deal was announced, the British government posted a press release that provides more detail:

  • Car export tariffs will reduce from 27.5% to 10%.
    • This will apply to a quota of 100,000 cars, almost the total the UK exported last year.
  • Steel and aluminum tariffs from 25% to 0%.
    • This will apply to all steel and aluminum leaving the UK.
  • Aerospace and defense firms will see some exemptions to tariffs.
  • Reciprocal market access for beef and other agricultural products.
    • This will apply to a quote of 13,000 tons of beef p.a.
    • There will be no change in food standards for imports to the UK, and maintaining bans on hormone-fed beef and chlorinated chicken.
  • UK to get preferential treatment on all future tariffs related to specific goods or sectors, such as pharmaceuticals.
  • No change on the contentious "Digital Services Tax" for big tech firms in the UK.

For context, cars were the number one deal to iron out, as they are the largest export from the UK to the U.S.

Overall, this seems like a good deal for both parties. The British were primarily concerned with automakers and steel leading up to this meeting, and they got tariff-free access for steel. The partnership with U.S.-UK over materials makes a lot of sense geopolitically, as UK materials firms are not doing the same kind of "dumping" that has led the U.S. to seek out protectionist policies for its domestic steel industry.

Likewise with vehicles, British manufacturers, like Jaguar Land Rover and Aston Martin (OTCPK:ARGGY), are in high demand in the U.S. Getting those tariffs down to the base case for nations, the 10% global tariff rate, is very helpful for these firms as it will make them more competitive than EU-based firms that are still facing the higher rates.

A deal was also struck for the U.S. to import more Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) engines and airplane parts in exchange for the UK importing $10B worth of Boeing (BA) aircraft.

One losing point for the U.S. was the no change status for the Digital Services Tax, which is a charge of 2% of revenue on big tech firms operating in the UK. This is one of the larger sticking points for U.S. tech firms that operate overseas, and was expected to have some carve-outs made in this deal.

While that didn't happen, that was the only "sour" part of the deal for the U.S. side. This appears as a win-win currently, which is very positive for several firms directly involved in these deals.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this first deal appeared to be a win for the U.S. and the UK. While the U.S. didn't get everything it wanted, one almost never does in negotiations. Instead, what it got was a fair and honest deal that is working to deepen economic ties with the U.S.'s closest ally, which is a very welcome change of rhetoric from the hawkishness we've seen from the Trump Administration on Europeans.

If this deal is indicative of future deals made with allied nations like the UK, the U.S. should remain in a solid position to bend where it needs to (0% tariffs on UK steel) and be stiff where it can be (no firm deals on pharmaceuticals, only "preference").

The full details of the trade agreement will be telling, once those see the light of day, but it may be some time before everything is set in stone. For now, these rules are going to go in effect, and have already impressed the market.

Two caveats to that positive note are:

  • This likely only applies to our allies, whom we are far more likely to give preferential treatment. China will definitely not be afforded the same luxuries, given the U.S. classifies them as an "adversarial nation." This will make reaching a deal with them far tougher than the UK, potentially dragging out negotiations for some time.
  • An EU-wide deal will be harder to reach because it has a trade deficit with the U.S., unlike the UK which started from a better position on trade accounts, something President Trump has said informs his view of who is "winning" a given trade relationship.

However, I remain positive that follow-up deals with fall along these lines. The goal of much of these trade deals is to isolate adversarial nations, but also to re-align markets of allies. These objectives are not mutually exclusive, per se.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Disney's Magic Is Spreading
data.description:
May 7th ended up being a really good day for shareholders of entertainment behemoth The Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS). After management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, shares shot up, closing higher by 10.8% for the day. Some of this upside can be attributed to the fact that the company had a very solid quarter compared to what analysts were anticipating. But I would argue that an even larger driver was news of additional investments into the company, including the firm's decision to open a new theme park. This is a monumental undertaking that, in the long run, can create a significant upside for the company.

For a long while now, I have been bullish about the firm. In March of this year, for instance, I dug into its theme park operations to show how what I call the ‘gem’ in the company's crown will continue to shine. In that article, I decided to reaffirm my ‘strong buy’ rating for the stock. For a while after that, shares were underperforming the broader market. But thanks to this recent jump, they are now up 2.8% compared to the 0.8% decline experienced by the S&P 500. I do think it is possible that the company could see some weakness in the near term because of the trade war situation that is going on. But for anybody focused on long-term investing, this might be the best company on the planet to consider buying into.

A great quarter

Pretty much, no matter how you stack it, the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a solid time for shareholders. Revenue for the company came in at $23.62 billion. This represents an increase of 7% compared to the $22.08 billion reported one year earlier. It also ended up being $470 million above what analysts were hoping to see. This rise in revenue was driven by all three of the companies' operating segments. Year over year, the Entertainment segment for the company saw revenue grow from $9.80 billion to $10.68 billion. The Sports segment saw growth from $4.31 billion to $4.53 billion. And finally, the Experiences segment grew from $8.39 billion to $8.89 billion.

On the bottom line, the picture was also solid. Earnings per share for the quarter totaled $1.81. That was up from the $0.01 loss reported the previous year and exceeded analysts’ expectations by $0.53. The data provided by management translated to arise from $20 million in the red to $3.28 billion in the green. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share went from $1.21 last year to $1.45 this year. This reading ended up being $0.24 above what analysts were hoping to see. Other profitability metrics for the company also improved. Operating cash flow shot up from $3.67 billion to $6.75 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a surge from $2.82 billion to $5.88 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $4.14 billion to $5.07 billion. It is worth noting that two of the three operating segments for the company saw improvements during the quarter. Entertainment reported a rise in profitability from $781 million to $1.26 billion. Experiences reported growth from $2.29 billion to $2.49 billion. The only weakness came from the Sports segment, which reported a drop in profitability from $778 million to $687 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. The solid second quarter of the year was instrumental in pushing revenue for the first half of this year up considerably compared to the same time of the previous year. Net income, adjusted net income, and cash flows all grew without exception. This is all evidence that Disney is a thriving and vibrant enterprise.

Checking out streaming

I have long believed that the streaming side of the business offers some of the strongest opportunities for growth. And according to management, the picture here is looking pretty good. During the second quarter of 2025, the number of Disney+ subscribers hit 126 million. That's up from the 120.8 million reported the previous year. The fastest growth, surprisingly, came from the company's domestic operations. This includes the US and Canada. Year over year, the number of subscribers jumped 7% from 54 million to 57.8 million. International subscribers made up the rest.

This is not the only streaming service that the company has. It also owns Hulu, which boasted 54.7 million subscribers as of the end of the most recent quarter. That's an increase of 9% compared to the 50.2 million reported the previous year. These numbers include the 4.4 million and 4.5 million, respectively, included under its Hulu + Live TV business that, as I wrote about in a previous article, is being merged with fuboTV (FUBO) in a multi-billion-dollar transaction that will leave Disney with a 70% ownership stake in the combined company. There's also ESPN+, which is now included under Disney’s Sports segment. It was the sole weak spot for the company, with the number of subscribers pulling back from 24.8 million to 24.1 million.

Pricing for the company has also been on the rise. Disney+ reported $7.77 in monthly revenue per paid subscriber. That compares favorably to the $7.13 per month reported the previous year. This might not seem like much of a difference. But when applied to the large number of paid subscribers that the company ended the quarter with, that year-over-year increase translates to an extra $968 million in annual revenue. Also, year over year, Hulu saw an increase in average revenue per paid subscriber from $19.32 per month to $19.40 per month. Even ESPN+ is doing well, with average revenue per paid subscriber expanding from $6.30 to $6.58.

Other important operations

Even though I think that the streaming side of the company is the most interesting at this time, there are other aspects of the business that deserve attention. Under the Experiences segment, we have the theme park operations of the business, resorts, and vacation assets, and a wide array of other revenue sources. Year over year, theme park admissions revenue increased by 4% from $2.81 billion to $2.92 billion. Management attributed this to a 4% increase in average per capita ticket price and to a 1% increase in attendance growth. Even more impressive was the revenue associated with its resorts and vacation activities. Over the last year, these sales skyrocketed 12.3% from $2.10 billion to $2.36 billion. A 6% rise caused by additional passenger cruise days, a 2% increase associated with higher unit sales at Disney Vacation Club, a 2% improvement from higher occupied hotel room nights, and a 1% increase stemming from higher average daily hotel room rates made this magic happen.

That's not all. Parks & Experiences' merchandise, food, and beverage revenue improved from $2.03 billion to $2.10 billion. Management claimed that this was the result of a 2% rise in higher average guest spending and a 1% increase in volume. Meanwhile, park licensing and other revenue grew from $568 million to $573 million. Even though it has more to do with the company's merchandising operations and retail business as opposed to its theme parks, its merchandise and licensing retail operations did see revenue grow by 5.4% from $889 million to $937 million. This was largely attributable to the release of a licensed game called Marvel Rivals.

Another important part of the company, even though it is a small part, is the theatrical distribution side of the business. I see this as the beginning of the content funnel for the firm that ultimately leads to additional growth opportunities. And the quarter was a resounding success. The business reported revenue of $646 million. That was massively higher than the $123 million reported the previous year. Management claims that this was because of the ongoing performance of both Mufasa: The Lion King and Moana 2, as well as the release of both Captain America: Brave New World and Snow White. By comparison, according to management, there were no significant titles released at the same time last year.

Shares are attractive

Management also had some interesting news regarding their outlook for the year. They currently anticipate that operating cash flow will be around $17 billion. That's actually $2 billion greater than what the company had previously forecasted. However, I don't think investors should factor in this $2 billion increase when analyzing the company. This is because it is the result of a deferral of tax payments that the company has been able to engineer. This means it will have to be paid eventually. Even so, if we strip this out, it would still be an improvement over the $13.97 billion reported for 2024.

Adjusted earnings per share are now forecasted to be around $5.75. Should this come to fruition, it would be 16% above what the company achieved in the previous year. It would also be above the $5.30 per share that management had previously forecast. Management went on to say that they continue to monitor macroeconomic developments because of what is going on with the trade war situation. I have written previously about how travel from overseas is down because of a rise in anti-American sentiment. But according to management, theme park bookings for the third quarter of this year are up 4% year over year and are 80% full. For the final quarter of the year, bookings are 7% higher than they were last year and are between 50% and 60% completed.

Assuming that's the kind of growth forecasted by management, it ends up coming to fruition, and shares are looking pretty good for such a high-quality company. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is priced using forward estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. And the great thing is that management decided to reaffirm their long-term guidance of double-digit earnings growth for both 2026 and 2027. So if we factor in future growth potential, the company should do just fine.

Management continues to make big moves

Also on May 7th, management announced that they are building a new Disney theme park and resort on Yas Island in Abu Dhabi. This is a tremendous and wealthy tourist market. According to management, the UAE (United Arab Emirates) is within a four-hour flight of a third of the world's population. It's the home to the largest global airline hub on the planet and sees 120 million passengers traveling through it each year. In 2023, the tourism industry in the UAE generated $52 billion in revenue. This came from 28 million international tourists visiting the country. Although that's not as high as the $131 billion in revenue generated in Florida that year, it is still a significant chunk of change that the company could benefit from.

Management said that this will be a waterfront resort with world-class entertainment and leisure that will particularly appeal to travelers from the Middle East, Africa, India, other parts of Asia, and Europe. Management is pitching this as a completely new and unique destination, unlike any that it currently has. Unfortunately, we do not yet know when this new theme park will open. Management has not provided a date. But it is highly probable that it will take a couple of years to get up and running.

We don't know how much money management will have to spend in order to get this theme park operational. But we do know that, separate from this, the company now has plans to invest over $30 billion in its Florida and California theme parks in order to ‘enhance offerings, create jobs, and support the US economy’. This includes expansions such as Villains Land and Cars Land, both of which are in Florida, and attractions such as Pandora and Coco in California.

The company is also in the process of making other investments and initiatives. I already mentioned the pending merger of Hulu + Live TV with fuboTV. In addition to this, the firm said that it expects to launch ESPN’s direct-to-consumer product in the next few months, but pricing and feature details have not been revealed. The company is also planning to fully integrate all three of its streaming platforms into a seamless user experience that management said will emphasize bundling and personalization. In addition to this, management also made a great move regarding debt. Net debt during the quarter came in at $37.04 billion. That's down $2.79 billion from the $39.82 billion reported one quarter earlier.

Takeaway

Almost without exception, I think that we can say that the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a great time for Disney and its investors. Even though shares are still nowhere near where I think they should be, this might be a blessing since it opens up the door for investors to buy in at cheaper prices. The company is doing really well, and I fully expect it to continue doing well moving forward. And when you consider how the stock is priced, I have no choice but to keep it as a ‘Strong Buy’ candidate.

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Market price returns reflect the midpoint of the bid/ask spread as of the close of trading on the exchange where Fund shares are listed. Market price returns do not represent the returns an investor would receive if they traded shares at other times. Brokerage commissions apply and will reduce returns.

Benchmark: Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index is a sub-index of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is a rules-based market value-weighted index of bonds with maturities of 1 year to 17 years engineered for the tax-exempt bond market. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

BLOOMBERG® and any Bloomberg Index are service marks of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates, including Bloomberg Index Services Limited ("BISL"), the administrator of the indices (collectively, "Bloomberg") and have been licensed for use for certain purposes by Hartford Funds. Bloomberg is not affiliated with Hartford Funds, and Bloomberg does not approve, endorse, review, or recommend any Hartford Funds product. Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff. The Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 0.39% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries increased from 68.4% to 77.4%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 88% over the last 20 years.1

Performance Summary

The Hartford Municipal Opportunities ETF underperformed the Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index during the quarter.

Security selection within investment-grade general-obligation bonds detracted.

Security selection in investment-grade and high-yield revenue bonds was the primary driver of positive performance. Within investment-grade revenue bonds, security selection in port, airport and marina, education, and industrial development contributed the most.

Duration* and yield-curve positioning negatively impacted relative returns, primarily due to the ETF's overweight exposure to the long-end of the curve, where yields rose significantly over the period.

Positioning & Outlook

Fundamentals are broadly positive after generous direct and indirect federal support and coming out of the pandemic.

Many municipal sectors have natural outlets for inflationary pressures, but the more labor- constrained sectors will be more challenged.

Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.

Tax-exempt municipal valuations as proxied by the municipal bond/treasury ratio are unattractive relative to history. However, municipal bond all-in yields look attractive relative to comparable-quality corporates at intermediate and long maturities.

Despite higher issuance this year, we expect demand to remain robust, particularly if rates begin to trend lower.

*Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of an investment’s price to nominal interest-rate movement.

1Source: Bloomberg

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

*Star Ratings: 3-year 4 stars out of 260 products, 5-year 4 stars out of 241 products for the period ended herein. Other share classes may have different ratings. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. data.title: Flex Q4: Strong Data Center Growth Still Not Enough To Offset Other Segments Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Flex Ltd. (NASDAQ:FLEX) recently reported its fiscal full-year results, which were very well received by the investing community, as its data center segments continue to grow at a fantastic pace. I wanted to go through the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the company’s outlook and why I am still a little hesitant, even if I think the company is set for good performance going forward.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, sales came in at $6.4B, up around 3.2% y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by $170m. Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.73, beating consensus estimates by 4 cents. Looking at the revenue segment breakdown, Agility Solutions came in at $3.5B, up a respectable 8.2%, while Reliability Solutions put a damper on the results, with a decline of 1.3%, to $2.9B.

Agility Solutions’ performance was driven by a strong demand for cloud and networking. For the year, data center revenue grew by approximately 50% y/y, indicating a robust demand for one of the highest-growth tech sectors right now. Around $5B of total revenue for the year was from data center revenues, which is outstanding. The segment also experienced some weakness in enterprise IT and consumer devices, but even then, the results were respectable.

Strength in Power partially offset the weakness in Reliability Solutions, so the result could have been worse than what the company reported.

Moving on to efficiency and profitability, gross margins improved to 8.8% from 8.4% last year, driven by favorable mix and operational efficiency, which is as vague as it gets. Adjusted operating margin also improved by around 70bps from last year to 5.7%, which reflects a strategic shift to higher-margin businesses, especially in the high-demand business segments like data center and cloud solutions, as well as power. Agility Solutions benefited from these tailwinds as the segment saw its margins improve by 100bps to 6.6%, which is a new record for the company. Even with a lackluster top-line performance by Reliability Solutions, the segment still saw margin improvements of around 40bps to 6.2% for the quarter.

The shift to focus on high-margin business is bearing fruit, and I often say that the top-line performance isn’t what matters to me. I would rather see the company doing better operationally.

Looking at the company’s financial situation, FLEX finished its fiscal year with $2.3B in cash and equivalents, against $2.5B in long-term debt. That’s a decent ratio between the two numbers, so I wouldn’t be too worried about the debt; however, just to make sure, let’s look at the company’s interest coverage ratio, which stood at around 6x. Well above what analysts consider safe (2x). So, no liquidity or insolvency issues here.

On top of these results, the management issued a revenue outlook for FY 26 to be in the range of $25B to $26.8B, with an adjusted operating margin target of 6% to 6.1%, and adjusted EPS of $2.81 to $3.01, which reflects continued confidence in the company’s strategy of expanding margins and growth in high-demand business segments like data centers and cloud.

Revenue guidance at the midpoint of $25.9B is higher than what analysts estimated by around $270m, while adjusted EPS was well above the estimates, $2.91 vs $2.61 a share.

Overall, the report has been more positive than not. The results were initially met negatively, which pushed the company’s share price down almost 5%. That didn’t last long, as the shares quickly recouped all the losses and then some, and are now up over 12% since the lows of the announcement of the quarter. The investors digested the report and saw that the beat on top and bottom lines, as well as a beat on guidance, is clearly a positive.

Comments on the Outlook

The elephant in the room and the main theme for the last couple of months is, of course, the impact of tariffs on companies like FLEX, which has one of the largest and most diversified supply chain networks out there. The company has around 100 manufacturing sites in around 30 different countries in Asia, Europe, and the Americas. This sort of footprint is quite advantageous for the company, as it means it can rapidly shift its production in case of some unforeseen change in demand, or geopolitical factors, as well as regulations.

So, a prime example of this is the tariffs. The management focused the earnings call on the implications of tariffs on the company and emphasized that the tariff impact on operations and guidance numbers is not included as of right now, which is understandable since we don’t know when the Trump administration is going to flip-flop again on this issue, which makes it hard to model internally and externally. However, the company’s CFO, Kevin Crum, said that if the current tariff environment remains, he expects a slight deterioration in operating margins vs. the guidance, but that this impact is going to be immaterial.

With such a diverse supply chain, the management aims to minimize the impact of the tariffs, and if needed, they will pass through some of the costs onto the customers, which will hardly be accretive to profit margins, calling them “low-calorie revenue.”

The automotive segment has been highlighted as one of the segments that will see some disruptions in the short run, given the targeted efforts by the administration; however, outside of this segment, the management doesn’t expect significant impacts to demand, which has been well-received by the investing community.

The continued strong demand in data centers and cloud seems to be the reason a lot of investors are rather bullish on the company right now, and for a good reason, in my opinion. The CapEx budgets on AI and data center infrastructure continue to balloon, even with some calling it unsustainable. The fact that most of the big tech companies aren’t slowing down in the AI race means there is a lot of room for growth for companies that are shifting their focus to this high-demand business segment.

I think the growth is going to remain here for a bit longer, but I don’t expect it to be this strong for a very long time. Eventually, companies will have to tone it down, and we will see a downcycle, but hopefully, by then, companies like FLEX will find other avenues to focus on and deploy capital to continue their growth, which right now doesn’t look like it’s very good, if we look at the headline numbers alone. The more the data center revenue makes up of the total revenue number, the higher the headline number will become, and I expect it to improve going forward, as long as the tariff situation doesn’t derail all the plans.

Closing Comments

There are a lot of positives considering the company’s outlook, especially within the data center segment. I am adding the company to my watchlist and will revisit once we get more clarity on the whole macroeconomic situation and how well the company’s other segments fared. The Flex Ltd. top-line growth right now doesn’t entice me, as well as the business’ tight margins, which may see a slight decline in the next couple of quarters. However, given its diversified supply chain, there is a high chance that everything will be just fine, which makes it quite enticing.

The company’s share price recovered a bit too quickly for me to jump in right now, so I will sit on the sidelines for now and observe the situation from the outside. I’m a little hesitant to start adding a company to my portfolio in such an uncertain environment.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, Y - 5/1/00; F - 2/28/17; I - 8/31/06; R3, R4, R5 - 12/22/06; R6 - 2/28/19. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 5/1/00. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: S&P Composite 1500 Health Care Index comprises those companies included in the S&P Composite 1500 that are classified as members of the GICS health care sector. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Top Ten Holdings (%)

Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY)

13.05

UnitedHealth Group, Inc. (UNH)

10.08

4.76

3.39

2.79

2.54

Cencora, Inc. (COR)

2.51

AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV)

2.45

Percentage Of Portfolio

48.47

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

The S&P Composite 1500 Health Care Index rose +5.54% during the quarter, outperforming the broader US market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index (-4.27%). Within the benchmark, healthcare-services stocks outperformed the most, while small-cap biopharma stocks underperformed during the period.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Healthcare Fund (I Share) underperformed the S&P Composite 1500 Health Care Index during the quarter.

Security selection was the driver of relative underperformance as selection within large-cap biopharma and medical technology detracted most.

Sub-sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock-selection process, detracted from relative performance. Allocation effect was driven by our overweight to mid-cap biopharma.

At the issuer level, our top-two relative detractors were an underweight to AbbVie and not owning CVS Health, while our top-two relative contributors were overweights to Alignment Healthcare and Cencora.

Positioning & Outlook

Biopharma remains a rich environment for innovation. We are excited about developments in GLP-1 and next-generation metabolic medicines, antibody drug conjugates for cancer treatment, and various modalities of genetic medicines. The range of outcomes for stocks remains wide as we await clarity on the US administration’s tariff and drug-pricing policies. US drug prices may gradually decrease over time, but we think pricing risks are manageable, and we find opportunities in companies with rich pipelines and track records of productive business development. We are monitoring organizational changes at the US Food and Drug Administration to evaluate their potential impact on the timing and standards of new drug approvals. We are underweight large-cap biopharma but selectively adding to companies with lower valuations, downside support and reaccelerating growth opportunities. Select SMID-cap biopharma companies remain well-positioned, and ultimately, the successful development of new drugs that address significant unmet medical needs should drive value creation.

We believe medical-technology innovation has never been stronger due to advancements in technology development, engineering capabilities, and integrated informatics. Areas of most interest include interventional cardiology, robotics, and a reacceleration in the bioproduction cycle. We remain disciplined around valuation and selective in our exposures.

Healthcare-services companies are well-positioned to help solve the challenge of rising healthcare costs and to benefit from the transition from a fee-for-service to a fee-for-value care system. We see attractive risk-reward in Medicare Advantage companies as valuations are low and profitability has troughed across government-insurance programs. A favorable final rate notice could be a positive catalyst. Providers should benefit from higher utilization and behavioral health companies have bipartisan political support in the US. We favor companies focused on improving patient outcomes while reining in costs and scaled players with rising market share.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Risks of focusing investments on the healthcare related sector include regulatory and legal developments, changes in funding or subsidies, patent and intellectual property considerations, intense competitive pressures, rapid technological changes, long and costly process for obtaining product approval by government agencies, potential product obsolescence, rising cost of medical products and services, and price volatility risk. • Small- and mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

data.title: Saturn Oil & Gas: Needing A Little Cost Work
data.description:
Saturn Oil & Gas Inc. (OTCQX:OILSF) is one of the companies that is taking advantage of some areas that have become competitive thanks to recent technology advances. However, costs here are on the higher side. But they do not appear to be high enough that the competitive edge is impaired. Sometimes this "higher" reflects "inherited" costs that will become less significant over time. Nonetheless, investors would be wise to compare what is going on here with lower cost competitors before making a decision. This is clearly a growth story where costs need some work; but they are also not out of control.

It should be noted that this company may have better liquidity in Canada than in the United States. It would also be suggested to use limit orders when considering purchasing the stock.

Debt

Like many other companies I follow, there is a definitive plan to decrease the debt. Should this be successful, it could lead to considerably higher stock prices than is the case right now. Mr. Market really wants a debt ratio of 1.0 or below and management has a plan to get it there.

(Note: Saturn Oil & Gas Is a Canadian Company That Reports Using Canadian Dollars.)

The current political uncertainty that threatens tariffs and trade wars may affect management's plans (through no fault of management). One of the issues with debt is that debt needs must be met no matter the industry conditions. However, it is also clear that management gave itself some slack so that a situation beyond its control (like the current one) can be handled without causing a financial crisis.

Net Debt And Free Cash Flow

Net debt at the end of the first quarter was roughly $C 814 million. Now that was done from C$ 860 million in December.

The debt ratio is slightly on the high side by United States standards. However, there was some free cash flow that did enable a small paydown of debt. Most Canadian companies generate more free cash flow in the second half of the fiscal year. The Canadian winter is usually a very busy time for capital expenditures. Then during Spring Breakup there is often a lot of repairs and maintenance.

Generally, any free cash flow in the first quarter is a good sign. That is especially true when the outlook for commodity prices is very weak right now.

The earnings press release has a very low capital expenditure for the second quarter due to Spring Breakup. Therefore, in this case, the free cash flow may be especially large in the second quarter.

Management emphasized that the advantage of Spring Breakup was the ability to review the budget and accompanying plans to make adjustments as needed for the weak commodity prices and current uncertainty as the future unfolds. Therefore, the majority of the budget is therefore very flexible. This is especially true before operations restart after Spring Breakup.

Exchange Risk Hedged

Now, this company issued United States dollar denominated debt; but it earns its money in Canadian dollars.

This strategy may well make sense as the Canadian dollar is currently regarded as cheap. There is some protection as noted above. However, this can be interpreted as another "gamble," however well placed in addition to the usual oil and gas risk that happens with an investment like this.

Board Experience

Now there is some general board experience in advising small growing companies

I would personally prefer to see more direct experience building and selling oil and gas companies. Both the board and senior management is a bit light in that area.

This may also account for the slightly higher per BOE costs that I am seeing. However, the goal to bring the debt in line may indicate that there is enough experience there for the company to succeed.

Area Of Operation

This Canadian operator is in some of the larger basin areas of Canada.

Saturn's Flat Lake acreage in Saskatchewan is a secondary recovery project. These tend to be very high oil percentage of production with slightly higher costs than either unconventional or conventional. Oftentimes, the higher costs are more than made up for by the low decline rates (that generate a lot of free cash flow) and the unusually large margins created by the high percentage of oil production.

However, the higher costs also make this a potential candidate to shut-in production to minimize negative cash flow during periods of weak commodity prices. Given the weaker commodity price outlook, we will have to see how this operation does in the near future.

Both the Cardium and Kaybob are well regarded light oil plays that are unusually low cost. Both areas are now producing more liquids (overall) than was the case in the past. Several areas were in fact previously known for their dry gas production. Some still are. What has happened in areas like the Cardium, Duvernay, and others is that intervals have been found that are liquids rich or are even considered oily that were not commercial to develop in the past.

Summary

Saturn Oil & Gas Inc. is a Canadian player with some elevated risk factors compared to other ideas in the industry. In this case, the company has United States denominated debt that is currently hedged for exchange rate risk factors.

Secondly, the debt levels are on the high side. However, the company does have a plan to repay the debt fast to bring the debt ratio down. But until that happens, the stock price may lag the competition.

Last, there are some secondary recovery operations that are likely used for cash generation but could turn out to be shut-in if the currently weak commodity price situation gets worse.

For this reason, Saturn Oil & Gas is probably a hold. I do not know that I would outright sell it if I had it. Management clearly has plans to improve the situation this fiscal year. That might be enough time to do the job. However, I also think I can find competition that is already there with roughly the same prospects.

For this size company, I do prefer management that has built and sold companies before. However, you do have to get started on that to build that reputation. Therefore, there are always newcomers that might succeed. Therefore, this management is worth watching for the progress made.

Nonetheless, I think the current strategy has a bit of extra risk to it compared to other companies. Therefore, this idea would appeal to those investors comfortable with that extra risk.

Risks

The elevated debt levels combined with some high-cost operations make this a bit risky during a period of uncertainty. That is before you get to the hedged but United Stated denominated debt while earnings are in Canadian dollars.

The current article was written with the ideas that the current tariff threats and potential trade wars are bargaining positions that will be replaced with something constructive, permanent, and predictable. However, there is no assurance that will happen.

Should something permanent not emerge, then the extra risk factors could prove to be a liability that would heighten the chances of failure.

A loss of key personnel could set the company's future back materially.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Singapore Technologies Engineering: After A 72% Rally, Is There More Fuel In The Tank?
data.description:
Singapore Technologies Engineering Ltd (OTCPK:SGGKF, OTCPK:SGGKY) or ST Engineering surged 72% since my first report and now trades at levels that value its forward earnings and bring the valuation closer to that of the peer group. In this report, I will discuss the full-year results and assess whether the stock is still a buy or whether it is time to sell.

For those that have not read my prior coverage of ST Engineering, I recommend reading the company description as well as an assessment of the risks and opportunities below. If you are familiar with the company already, you can skip to the discussion of the orders and revenues.

What Does Singapore Technologies Engineering Do?

Singapore Technologies Engineering is a company with a $18.3 billion market cap and a wide range of products and services.

The company is active in the following end markets:

  • Aerospace
  • Defense
  • Smart City
  • Marine
  • Digital Tech

So, if you are searching for a company with a broad product portfolio, STE definitely ticks the boxes. The Aerospace segment consists of Aerostructures and Systems, which includes cabin interior outfitting, freighter conversion, composite panel manufacturing and nacelle manufacturing. The MRO segment focuses on aircraft and engine services, and the portfolio of supported platforms includes the CFM LEAP and CFM56 engines, which are the most popular turbofans in operation today. The third and final component of the aerospace business is the aircraft and engine trading business, where ST Engineering trades and leases engines and aircraft.

The Defense and Public Security segment includes Defense Aerospace, Digital Systems & Cyber, Land Systems and Marine. That is a big area of operations with land, air, and sea solutions as well as C5ISR, homeland security, training, cybersecurity, data, AI, and cloud solutions. The final segment is Urban Solutions and Satcom, which includes smart solutions for rail, road and urban infrastructure as well as satellite communications.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For ST Engineering?

I previously listed the following risks for ST Engineering:

  • A tough environment for satellite communication and equipment.
  • Parts shortages for aircraft and engine maintenance shops are increasing turnaround times.

Since my prior coverage, I am adding a third risk, and that is the trade war. If the trade war propagates, that could result in higher material and parts costs that affect the business of ST Engineering.

There are also opportunities. For the satcom business, there won’t be any growth overnight. However, the company is actively managing the costs. For the MRO part of the business, there are shortages of parts but original equipment manufacturers such as The Boeing Company (BA), Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) and Embraer are facing similar challenges. The result is that production rates cannot keep up with demand for airplanes and that increases demand for maintenance activities as more airlines are sending their in-fleet airplanes in for maintenance instead of having retiring airplanes.

In the freighter business, there is still an opportunity for conversion activities in support of e-commerce demand and those freighter conversions should have nice margins. I do have to add a sidenote that in the near term, this part of the business may become pressured in the absence of constructive trade resolutions.

For the defense business, the current demand environment will also drive higher volumes, which would allow for better fixed and variable cost absorption.

ST Engineering Sales And Earnings Grow On Favorable End Market Exposure

Revenues grew by 12% in 2024 to S$11.3 billion with 15% growth in the defense market and 10% growth in the commercial end markets. We see that the defense and public security sales accounted for 44% of the sales followed by commercial aerospace with a 39% share and urban solutions and satcom comprised the remaining 17%. While customers are concentrated in Asia, we do see that Europe and the US also account for a significant portion of the sales.

With the trade turmoil, one could think that there may be some pressure on demand, especially in Asia where some countries are hit hard by tariffs, but I do believe that overall, ST Engineering has a nicely diversified customer base.

The Commercial Aerospace segment saw sales grow 15% to S$4.4 billion on a recurring business basis meaning that aircraft sales which are lumpy are excluded from the totals. The growth was driven by engine MRO, passenger-to-freighter conversions, nacelle, and composite panel sales. EBIT grew 19% to S$400 million on higher volumes providing better fixed cost amortization and improved margins on the freighter conversions.

The Defense and Public Security segment saw sales increase 16% to S$4.94 billion with widespread growth of all parts of the business resulting in a 15% comparable growth in EBIT to S$636 million. The Urban Solutions and Satcom business saw relatively flat sales at S$1.96 billion where urban solutions sales were largely offset by lower satcom sales. EBIT, however, improved significantly driven by the positive contribution of the urban solutions revenue growth to the margins and lower losses in the satcom business as the company is managing costs, had lower severance costs and a divestment loss booked last year did obviously not occur again this year. As a result, EBIT increased from S$10 million to S$40 million.

Order Book Continues Growing

While the order book growth did decrease in 2024, it is still a very healthy order book valued at S$28.5 billion or more than two times its FY24 sales. Around 30% of the backlog is expected to convert to revenues this year. In September, I expected the company to exit the year with at least S$25 billion in its backlog, and it exceeded that level significantly. So, we see strong sales growth in 2024 and a strong backlog that supports additional growth to sales in the years ahead.

Singapore Technologies Engineering Stock Remains A Buy On Peer Group Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The stock price is based on the SGGKY listing representing 10 ordinary shares.

With the increased focus on defense, ST Engineering stock has surged, and also the commercial aviation market is an attractive market to be active in. That justifies an expansion of the EV/EBITDA multiple to the peer group.

My projections are based on a gradual decrease in the debt, 8% growth in EBITDA and relatively stable free cash flow generation when looking at the compounded FCF. For 2025 and 2026, the EBITDA estimates have been revised up by around 8% with free cash flow estimates increasing along those lines as well. We see that the stock is currently valued according to FY23 levels, the FY24 supported price target would imply 16% upside with a $67.92 stock price target and if the stock were to trade in line with FY25 earnings the price target would increase to $74.55. That is more than enough to justify a buy rating with a conservative target of $67.92.

Conclusion: ST Engineering Still Offers Value

ST Engineering does face some potential challenges in the satellite market and the freighter conversion market may soften if the trade war continues. However, the company has exposure to two markets with good growth prospects and which support continued growth for the business and share prices. As a result, I believe a buy rating, even after the significant increase in share prices, is justified.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
